content:
Is this happening to all messages or just the one?

What is your anti-virus, if any?

Please show a screen shot of the filter window so we can see how it's constructed.

The "0x8000FFFF" error is usually or always a Windows error that may be passed on to you by Thunderbird. Unfortunately, it's (intentionally?) unspecific, but can mean a file can't be read from or written to disk. In this case, I suspect that it's being held by your AV. This can be tested by temporarily disabling the AV, or by creating an exception for your Thunderbird Profile in your AV, or by restarting Windows in Safe Mode with Networking, which will temporarily disable the AV.

Another possibility is that the message may have been flagged as suspicious or infected by your AV and may have been quarantined. This could trigger the error message you see. Check your AV quarantine feature or log for any sign of it.
creator: rednoise

content:
Well, I recreated this filter, and it works without errors. Yours is confusing because there is no explicit "Move" action called for, yet that seems to be what's triggering the error message. This makes me wonder if you have other message filters that aren't showing? Otherwise, maybe the filter is corrupt - I would delete it and re-create it.

Other things to try:

- restart Thunderbird in Troubleshooting Mode (see Help | Troubleshooting Mode) and see if still happens;

- you don't mention what version of Thunderbird you're using, but maybe an update would fix it;

- This doesn't address the problem itself, but since you're always deleting messages from those senders, consider setting up filters for them on the server so you won't even have to download them;

- These appear to be legitimate senders who would honor unsubscribe requests.

Otherwise I'm out of ideas.

content:
I didn't realize that the list was longer than what you showed. Maybe there's an operational limit to how many entries there can be. You could split the list into smaller chunks, at least as a test.

You are correct about the danger of carelessly sending unsubscribe responses to ads, and if you're not in direct control maybe it would be too much to ask of the user, but I bet many of these would respond to the request. You would have to examine the original email, though to confirm their legitimacy.

Blindly blocking unwanted spam address like this is rarely effective. Real spammers rarely if ever use the same From address twice, so you end up with huge blacklists that do nothing. The blacklist is only effective against senders who always use the same From:, which are more often legit companies than spammers. Thunderbird's built in Adaptive Junk Filter feature, while it can never be 100% effective, does a better job with less user intervention than regular Message Filters.

https://support.mozilla.org/en-US/kb/thunderbird-and-junk-spam-messages

content:
Thunderbird is the email client program. It's not a server, so emails do not get migrated to Thunderbird. Emails get downloaded into the mail account. Pop accounts can only access the server 'Inbox' and download emails to pop account 'Inbox'. There is no synchronisation between any pop account folder and server folder. Emails are stored on computer and independent of server. Imap accounts synchronise imap folders with server folders. Imap accounts can only display whatever is on the server. Emails are stored on the server. Even if emails are downloaded, you should not believe you have an independent copy because it can only display what is on server. But it can be used to create backups that are independent of server.

Thunderbird has mail accounts which are for existing email addresses created with eg: ISP or Business or free ones with eg: gmail.

An 'Inbox' is in a mail account set up as either Pop or Imap protocol. There is also a default account called 'Local Folders' which can store emails independently of any mail account on your computer.

You seem to have an existing email address with LSU.edu. Do you mean you created a mail account for that LSU.edu. email address in Thunderbird ? Did you set it up as a Pop or IMAP account ?

You say " only 7179 are accessible (blue button),remaining 28,012 are listed in a greyed out button. "

It seems very odd because no emails are in 'buttons'.

The left pane is called the Folder Pane. It contains the name of the mail account and it contains folders which contain emails. At the bottom with be the 'Local Folders' account which usually has an Inbox, Trash and the 'Outbox' which is used by all account when 'Send Later' is used.

It would be helpful, if you could upload an image showing what you see, so we can visualise the problem. The image needs to be in an image file eg: jpg

content:
re : I cannot find any important messages from my Inbox.

What do you mean by 'important' ?

Thunderbird has something called 'Tags' and one of them is called 'Important' If your emails have 'important' tags, they usually do get synchronised.

Menu icon (3 lines) > 'View' > 'Folders' > select 'Tags' It opens in the left Folder Pane - usually below the 'Local Folders' account. The tags can appear empty until you click on 'Important' then Thunderbird scans emails to find out what if any have the Important Tag.

However, eg: gmail offers something called 'Important' label. All of gmails 'labels' work like tags to give a view like regular folders.

So if you have set up an IMAP account, maybe you have a folder on the server called 'Important'. In Folder Pane

  • Right click on the mail account name and select 'Subscribe'.
  • click on 'Refresh'
  • Select all server folders
  • click on 'OK'

content:
re : And finally, it appears that all the messages that did come over (both read and unread) are from my Trash or Deleted folders from my LSU account.

You say 'Trash or Deleted folders' - clarify please.

Some people have a folder called 'Trash' and some have a folder called 'Deleted' and some have a folder called 'Bin' and there are other variants, but all are the 'Trash' a place for unwanted mail that needs to be completely removed.

OR did you have some folders which you created for organising emails and decided to delete the folder and all it's contents.


It does seem odd to have 28,012 messages or more as you say some years do not appear, stored in the Trash. Trash is for unwanted mail. Generally cleared out on a regular basis eg: once a month. I would have expected you to have completely emptied the TRash before synchronising emails - why download stuff you do not want ?

OR did you move them out of TRash into the Inbox?

If it was a Pop account, then Thunderbird can only access the server Inbox and download what is in it into the pop account Inbox. So those emails in a Pop Inbox had to be in a server Inbox.

If it was an IMAP account, then each imap folder synchronises with server folder of same name and downloads whatever is in it. Imap is just a mirror copy of the server. It seems odd that you do not have many folders displaying especially for an imap account.

Please tell us what you see in this location:

  • In 'Account Settings'
  • Select 'Server Settings' for the account
  • On the right - 'Server type' does it say 'Pop Mail Server' OR 'Imap Mail server' ?
  • You can always post an image if it helps.

content:
Thanks for info. You have an existing email address with LSU.edu. LSU.edu seem to use the Microsoft outlook.office365 server.

All the LSU emails are stored on the outlook.office365 server.

You probably have a Microsoft Account which you can use to access the webmail account of that email address via a browser.

In Thunderbird, You have an IMAP mail account for that email address and it's set up to access the emails stored on the outlook.office365 server. Those emails may be fully downloaded or just headers downloaded - we would need to check settings in order to be sure.

IMAP accounts should never be considered as independent from the server because they can only display whatever is on the server. Imap accounts are a mirror copy of what is on the server. If you access the webmail account and delete anything, then you will find those emails will auto be deleted from the Imap account.

re your comment "I recently migrated all my LSU.edu emails over to Thunderbird." You have not 'migrated' your emails to Thunderbird. Thunderbird is able to display your LSU emails by being allowed to access the stored emails on server. Providing you are downloading full copies of everything - we would need to check settings for this - then you can use Thunderbird to create a backup that is independent of server. You can also get 'Copies' of emails and put them in the 'Local Folders' account - that is independent of server and they are only stored on your computer.

As you have an IMAP account, you should be able to see a lot more folders.

  • In Folder Pane
  • Right click on the name of the imap mail account folder and select 'Subscribe' - see image below as guide
  • Click on 'Refresh' button
  • Select all the checkboxes for all the folders
  • click on 'OK'
creator: Toad-Hall

content:
You will have trouble finding someone with that version to compare with your description. If you're determined to stick with the 68x series, I would recommend that you at least upgrade to the latest and last version in that line, 68.12.1, which has the most refinements and bug fixes.

https://releases.mozilla.org/pub/thunderbird/releases/68.12.1/

This sounds to me like a glitchy interaction with your computers display subsystem, The first thing I'd try would be to disable hardware acceleration in Thunderbird, which can sometimes cause display anomalies like this. You can find that setting (in more recent versions) at <Settings | General (or Advanced on some old versions) | Use hardware acceleration when available>. Uncheck that option to disable. It's intended to improve performance, but I find that it makes little difference in practice.

content:
- First, install Thunderbird and run it for the first time. If it starts prompting you to set up an email account, decline all offers. When it finally settles down, exit Thunderbird.,

- If you have a backed-up copy of the "C:\Users\(your login name)\AppData\Roaming\Thunderbird" folder, the one that contains your profile(s), copy (not move) it to the equivalent location, overwriting the new, incomplete "..\Thunderbird\" folder. If all goes well, when you next start Thunderbird, everything should be as it was when you made the backup. Depending on how old the backup copy is and if from an earlier version, you may need to reset some preferences and re-enter you passwords.

content:
What version of Thunderbird? Is this an IMAP account or POP3?

Generally speaking, Sent emails should never go directly into Trash unless you have a very good reason to set that as an option. The Thunderbird setting for where to place copies of sent email can be found at

<Account Settings | (the account in question) | Copies & Folders>

Confirm that the "When sending messages, automatically: Place a copy in:" setting is enabled and appropriate.

If IMAP, also check the provider's web mail interface to see if the missing Sent copies are there and that new ones are placed there.

content:
The port is associated with the Security setting and is set by the server administrator, so it's not relevant in this case. You can see if your account is set up as IMAP or POP3 by looking at <Account Settings | (the account in question) | Server Settings>. At the top it will say "Server Type" is either IMAP Mail Server or POP Mail Server.

FYI, the difference between the two is that with IMAP all email is stored both on the server and on you local computer. This is an advantage because there are always at least two copies of everything, and you can access the mailbox from multiple devices. With POP, once you download messages, the only copy is the one on your computer.

Until you're sure what's going on, you can create a Local Folder and set Thunderbird to put copies of Sent Mail there. But keep in mind that even if there's a copy in Sent, that's no real guarantee that it was actually SENT by the server. There's a certain level of trust you have to have. If in doubt, and you have other email accounts, like Gmail or whatever, you can send yourself test messages.

Rogers set it so email access passwords had to be upgraded to their easy-peesy to hack email password. Only letter - upper and lower. A reason to check with ISP before Thunderbird.

All the time, all email pickup/delivery was by Thunderbird. Suggestion: Check with your ISP before suspecting Thunderbird. Yes, Thunderbird might have it's own problems, but I have come to believe Thunderbird creators goal is first, to protect your Rights. The ISPs want all the info they can get from your WEBmail so they can use targeted advertising in your Webmail GUI/Dashboard and in your webmail's to others. With Thunderbird, you have a excellent tool with over 22 years experience in building Thunderbird with your Right to Privacy first on the agenda.

Email (not talking webmail) issues? Try your ISP first.

Just say'n s'all
